parentQuestion: A patient can't turn their head to the left or shrug their left shoulder. Which nerve is damaged?

scaffolds.q: Which cranial nerve controls the muscles for head turning and shoulder shrugging?
scaffolds.explain: CN XI (accessory nerve) controls the sternocleidomastoid muscle (head turning) and trapezius muscle (shoulder shrugging). It's the only cranial nerve with this specific job.

wrongExplains.0: CN X (vagus) controls heart, lungs, gut, and voice. It doesn't control skeletal muscles for head turning or shoulder movement.
wrongExplains.2: CN XII (hypoglossal) controls TONGUE muscles only. Head and shoulder movements use completely different muscles controlled by CN XI.
wrongExplains.3: CN VII (facial) controls facial EXPRESSION muscles (smiling, frowning). It doesn't control neck or shoulder muscles.

scaffolds.q: The sternocleidomastoid (SCM) muscle does which action?
scaffolds.explain: The sternocleidomastoid TURNS the head. It runs from the sternum and clavicle up to the mastoid process (behind the ear). When one side contracts, it turns your head to the OPPOSITE side.

wrongExplains.0: Shoulder shrugging is done by the TRAPEZIUS muscle (also controlled by CN XI). The SCM specifically handles head rotation.
wrongExplains.2: Opening the mouth involves jaw muscles (masseter, pterygoids) controlled by CN V. The SCM is a neck muscle for head rotation.
wrongExplains.3: Raising eyebrows uses the frontalis muscle controlled by CN VII (facial). The SCM is nowhere near the forehead.

scaffolds.q: The trapezius muscle does which action?
options: Turns the head side to side
scaffolds.explain: The trapezius SHRUGS the shoulders (elevates them toward the ears). It's a large diamond-shaped muscle running from the skull down to the mid-back. CN XI controls it.

wrongExplains.0: Head turning is done by the STERNOCLEIDOMASTOID (also controlled by CN XI). The trapezius is primarily for shoulder elevation.
wrongExplains.2: Tongue movement is controlled by CN XII (hypoglossal). The trapezius is a back/shoulder muscle with no tongue involvement.
wrongExplains.3: Closing the eyes uses the orbicularis oculi controlled by CN VII. The trapezius is a back/shoulder muscle, not a facial muscle.

scaffolds.q: Here's the tricky part: To turn your head LEFT, which SCM muscle contracts?
scaffolds.explain: The RIGHT SCM turns your head LEFT! This is counterintuitive. The muscle pulls from one side, rotating your head to look the opposite way. Right muscle = look left. Left muscle = look right.

wrongExplains.0: The left SCM turns your head RIGHT, not left. The muscle pulls from behind, so it turns your face away from the contracting side.
wrongExplains.2: For pure rotation, one SCM contracts while the other relaxes. Both contracting together would flex the neck forward, not turn it.
wrongExplains.3: The SCM is THE muscle for head turning. While other muscles assist, the SCM is the primary rotator controlled by CN XI.

scaffolds.q: If the LEFT CN XI is damaged, the patient can't turn their head to which side?
scaffolds.explain: Left CN XI damage = can't turn head RIGHT. Remember the crossover: Left SCM turns the head right. So left nerve damage means the left SCM is weak, and rightward head turning fails.

wrongExplains.0: Turning left uses the RIGHT SCM (controlled by right CN XI). Left CN XI damage affects rightward turning, not leftward turning.
wrongExplains.2: Only one side is affected because each CN XI controls its own side's muscles. The other side's muscles still work normally.
wrongExplains.3: Without the left SCM working, the patient definitely can't turn their head to the right as strongly. The weakness is real.

scaffolds.q: In this patient who can't turn left or shrug the left shoulder, which CN XI is damaged?
options: CN XI is not the problem
scaffolds.explain: RIGHT CN XI is damaged. Remember the crossover: To turn LEFT, you need the RIGHT SCM. And the shoulder shrug weakness is on the SAME side as the nerve damage. Right nerve = can't turn left + can't shrug right shoulder.

wrongExplains.1: Left CN XI damage would prevent turning the head RIGHT (not left) because the left SCM turns the head rightward. The symptoms don't match left-sided damage.
wrongExplains.2: If both were damaged, the patient couldn't turn OR shrug on EITHER side. This patient has one-sided symptoms, suggesting one-sided damage.
wrongExplains.3: These are classic CN XI symptoms — head turning + shoulder shrug weakness. The combination screams CN XI damage.

scaffolds.q: Why is CN XI called the 'accessory' nerve?
options: It was discovered as an 'addition' to CN X
scaffolds.explain: CN XI was historically considered an 'accessory' (addition) to the vagus nerve (CN X) because they exit near each other and share some fibers. It's not 'optional' — it's essential for head and shoulder movement.

wrongExplains.0: CN XI is absolutely needed! Without it, you can't turn your head or shrug your shoulders properly. 'Accessory' refers to its anatomical relationship to CN X, not its importance.
wrongExplains.2: CN XI works all the time in healthy people. It's not intermittent. The name has nothing to do with reliability.
wrongExplains.3: The nerve has nothing to do with clothing or accessories. The anatomical term 'accessory' means it was seen as an addition to another structure.

scaffolds.q: A doctor tests CN XI by asking the patient to do what TWO movements?
options: Shrug shoulders and turn head against resistance
scaffolds.explain: The doctor tests SHOULDER SHRUG and HEAD TURNING against resistance. If the patient can't resist the doctor pushing against these movements, CN XI may be damaged.

wrongExplains.0: Smiling and closing eyes tests CN VII (facial nerve). Different nerve, different test.
wrongExplains.2: Sticking out the tongue tests CN XII (hypoglossal). Swallowing involves CN IX and X. Neither tests CN XI.
wrongExplains.3: Looking up and sideways tests the eye movement nerves (CN III, IV, VI). Eye movements have nothing to do with CN XI.

scaffolds.q: CN XI is classified as purely ___.
scaffolds.explain: CN XI is purely MOTOR — it only carries commands TO muscles (SCM and trapezius). It doesn't carry any sensory information back to the brain. The purely motor cranial nerves are CN III, IV, VI, XI, and XII.

wrongExplains.0: Purely sensory nerves carry information TO the brain (like CN I, II, VIII). CN XI carries motor commands FROM the brain to muscles.
wrongExplains.2: Mixed nerves have both sensory and motor fibers (like CN V, VII, IX, X). CN XI is simple — just motor output to two muscles.
wrongExplains.3: Autonomic functions (heart rate, digestion) are mainly handled by CN X (vagus). CN XI controls voluntary skeletal muscles, not autonomic functions.
